figures.caption: Fig. 1. A schematic diagram depicting the the proposed ToMPC planner, where the ADMM solves the optimization problem in a distributed fashion.

Fig. 4. Simulation results for Scenario #1. Top: Minimum distances to the obstacle (Fig. 3 Left). The FDDP and ADMM methods are compared under different parameter settings. Bottom: Cartesian trajectories for all six test cases.
Fig. 6. Experimental results for Scenario #2. Top: Cartesian trajectory in the X-Y plane. Middle: Norm of Cartesian trajectory errors. Bottom: Minimum distance to the ball obstacle.

Fig. 8. Scenario #3: Evaluation of environment interaction. Left: Experimental setup. Right: Sensor frame and contact frame relationship. Notably, the F/T sensor is solely used for evaluation, without being involved in feedback control.

Fig. 7. Joint torque of the ToMPC in Scenario #2. Top: MPC torque τmpc, final desired torque τdes, and measured torque τ for the second joint. Bottom: Percentage of the feedforward torque in the total command.

Fig. 9. Force measurement results for Scenario #3. The diagrams from top to bottom correspond to the frequencies w=0.4, 0.8, 1.5, respectively. The left grey-shaded area is magnified in the right diagram.
Fig. 2. Illustration of interaction model (left) and task-oriented avoidance (right).

Fig. 10. Scenario #4: The robot wipes a whiteboard with a contact force of 8 N. Top: Experimental snapshots of the oaMPC and the ToMPC methods. Bottom: Rainbow plots illustrating the Cartesian trajectory and measured contact force.
